name: North America
description: Cities, landmarks, and trivia of the United States, Canada, and Mexico

questions.q: How many U.S. Presidents' faces are carved into Mount Rushmore?

source.notes: Washington, Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Lincoln.

questions.q: How many stars are on the U.S. flag?

source.notes: One for each of the 50 states.

questions.q: Which leaf is at the center of the Canadian national flag?

source.notes: 11-pointed stylized red maple leaf, adopted 1965.

questions.q: What is the capital of the United States?

source.notes: District of Columbia; federal enclave between Maryland and Virginia.

questions.q: The Pentagon, the headquarters of the U.S. Department of Defense, is a building with how many sides?

source.notes: Located in Arlington County, Virginia; completed 1943.

questions.q: Niagara Falls straddles the border between the U.S. and which other country?

source.notes: Between New York state and Ontario.

questions.q: What animal serves as the national bird of the United States?

source.notes: Adopted as the national emblem in 1782.

questions.q: What were the Hawaiian Islands originally called by British explorers in the 1770s?

source.notes: Named by Captain James Cook in 1778 for his patron, the 4th Earl of Sandwich.

questions.q: On what date does Mexico celebrate its Independence Day (often confused with Cinco de Mayo)?

source.notes: Commemorates Miguel Hidalgo's 1810 'Grito de Dolores'.

questions.q: Who was the last Aztec emperor, captured by the Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés?

source.notes: Also spelled Montezuma II; died 1520.

questions.q: Banff National Park, Canada's oldest national park, is in which province?

source.notes: Established 1885; part of the Canadian Rockies UNESCO site.

questions.q: Moraine Lake, famed for its vivid turquoise color, is located in which national park?

source.original_a: Moraine Lake (located in Banff National Park)
source.notes: Fed by Wenkchemna Glacier; featured on the old Canadian $20 bill.

questions.q: Artist Frida Kahlo, known for vivid self-portraits, was from which country?

source.notes: Born in Coyoacán, Mexico City in 1907.

questions.q: Tacos, now eaten worldwide, are a traditional food from which country?

source.notes: A staple of Mexican cuisine for centuries.

questions.q: Prince Edward Island in Canada inspired which famous novel by Lucy Maud Montgomery?

source.notes: Published 1908; set in the fictional Avonlea.

questions.q: Hawaii became the 50th U.S. state in which year?

source.notes: Admitted August 21, 1959, about seven months after Alaska.

questions.q: Mount Rushmore is located in which U.S. state?

source.notes: Carved into the Black Hills of South Dakota.

questions.q: The St. Lawrence River flows between Canada and which U.S. state?

source.notes: Forms part of the Ontario/New York border.

questions.q: Sable Island, home to wild horses and shipwrecks, is sometimes called the 'Galapagos of the North' and lies off the coast of which Canadian province?

source.notes: About 300 km east-southeast of Halifax.

questions.q: The historic transcontinental railway completed in 1885 that connects eastern and western Canada is called what?

source.notes: Final spike driven November 7, 1885 at Craigellachie, BC.

questions.q: Ulysses S. Grant rose to fame leading the Union forces in which war before becoming the 18th U.S. President?

source.notes: Accepted Lee's surrender at Appomattox Court House in 1865.

questions.q: Which U.S. city's cable cars are the only National Historic Landmarks that can actually move?

source.notes: In operation since 1873; three lines still run today.
